title: Ouvrir les fichiers locaux référencés dans les descriptions
description:
Permettre d'ouvrir les fichiers locaux (images, documents) référencés dans les descriptions d'issues en cliquant dessus.

## Contexte
Actuellement, les URLs externes (http/https) dans les descriptions sont cliquables et s'ouvrent dans le navigateur. Cependant, les chemins de fichiers locaux (ex: `.temp/screenshots/image.png`) ne sont pas interactifs.

## Fonctionnalité attendue
- Détecter les chemins de fichiers locaux dans le texte markdown
- Les rendre cliquables (même style que les liens externes)
- Au clic, ouvrir le fichier avec l'application par défaut du système :
  - Images (.png, .jpg, .gif) → Preview (macOS) / Visionneuse (Windows)
  - PDF → lecteur PDF par défaut
  - Autres fichiers → application associée

## Implémentation suggérée
1. Étendre le composant `LinkifiedText.vue` pour détecter les patterns de chemins de fichiers
2. Utiliser l'API Tauri `shell.open()` pour ouvrir les fichiers locaux
3. Résoudre les chemins relatifs par rapport au projet courant

## Patterns à détecter
- Chemins relatifs : `./path/to/file.png`, `path/to/file.png`
- Chemins absolus : `/Users/.../file.png`
- Extensions communes : `.png`, `.jpg`, `.jpeg`, `.gif`, `.pdf`, `.md`

## Critères d'acceptation
- [ ] Les chemins de fichiers sont détectés et rendus cliquables
- [ ] Le clic ouvre le fichier avec l'application système par défaut
- [ ] Les chemins relatifs sont résolus par rapport au projet actif
- [ ] Style visuel cohérent avec les liens externes (mais distinguable)

title: Système de rappels et notifications pour les issues
description:
Implémenter un système permettant aux utilisateurs de définir des rappels sur leurs issues et de recevoir des notifications desktop lorsque des échéances approchent.

## Contexte

Actuellement, les utilisateurs doivent manuellement vérifier leurs issues pour suivre les échéances. Un système de rappels permettrait une meilleure gestion du temps et une productivité accrue.

## Bénéfices attendus

- Meilleure visibilité sur les échéances
- Notifications proactives évitant les oublis
- Gestion personnalisée des rappels par issue

---

# ÉTAPE 1 : Modèle de données et stockage des rappels

## Objectif
Créer la structure de données pour stocker les rappels associés aux issues.

## Plan détaillé

### 1.1 Définir le schéma du rappel
- `id`: identifiant unique (UUID)
- `issueId`: référence vers l'issue Beads
- `reminderDate`: date/heure du rappel (ISO 8601)
- `message`: message personnalisé (optionnel, max 200 caractères)
- `isTriggered`: booléen indiquant si le rappel a été déclenché
- `createdAt`: date de création du rappel

### 1.2 Créer le fichier de stockage
- Utiliser un fichier JSON local dans `.beads/reminders.json`
- Structure : `{ "reminders": [...] }`
- Implémenter les fonctions CRUD côté Rust dans `src-tauri/src/lib.rs`
- Gérer la création automatique du fichier s'il n'existe pas

### 1.3 Ajouter les commandes Tauri
- `reminder_create(issue_id, reminder_date, message)` → Reminder
- `reminder_list(issue_id?)` → Vec<Reminder>
- `reminder_get(id)` → Reminder
- `reminder_update(id, reminder_date?, message?)` → Reminder
- `reminder_delete(id)` → bool

### 1.4 Wrapper TypeScript
- Créer `app/utils/reminder-api.ts`
- Typer les interfaces Reminder en TypeScript
- Exposer les fonctions async pour le frontend

## Critères d'acceptation
- [ ] Schéma Reminder défini et documenté
- [ ] Fichier reminders.json créé automatiquement
- [ ] Toutes les commandes Tauri fonctionnelles
- [ ] Wrapper TypeScript avec types complets

---

# ÉTAPE 2 : Interface utilisateur pour la gestion des rappels

## Objectif
Permettre à l'utilisateur de créer et gérer ses rappels depuis l'interface.

## Plan détaillé

### 2.1 Composant de sélection de date/heure
- Créer `app/components/ui/DateTimePicker.vue`
- Utiliser les composants shadcn-vue (Calendar + Input time)
- Gérer les fuseaux horaires avec date-fns-tz
- Validation : date doit être dans le futur
- Format d'affichage localisé

### 2.2 Dialog de création de rappel
- Créer `app/components/reminder/ReminderDialog.vue`
- Props : `issueId`, `isOpen`, `onClose`
- Champs : date/heure, message optionnel
- Boutons : Annuler, Créer
- Feedback visuel lors de la sauvegarde

### 2.3 Bouton dans la vue issue
- Modifier `app/components/issue/IssueDetail.vue`
- Ajouter icône cloche (Bell) dans la barre d'actions
- Tooltip : "Ajouter un rappel"
- Ouvrir ReminderDialog au clic

### 2.4 Liste des rappels dans le dashboard
- Créer `app/components/reminder/ReminderList.vue`
- Afficher : issue title, date rappel, message, actions
- Tri par date de rappel (plus proche en premier)
- Actions : modifier, supprimer
- État vide : "Aucun rappel configuré"

### 2.5 Indicateur visuel sur les issues
- Ajouter badge Bell sur les cards/lignes d'issues ayant un rappel
- Couleur différente si rappel proche (< 24h)
- Tooltip avec date du prochain rappel

## Critères d'acceptation
- [ ] DateTimePicker fonctionnel et accessible
- [ ] Dialog de création avec validation
- [ ] Bouton intégré dans IssueDetail
- [ ] Liste des rappels dans le dashboard
- [ ] Indicateurs visuels sur les issues

---

# ÉTAPE 3 : Système de notifications desktop

## Objectif
Déclencher des notifications natives quand un rappel arrive à échéance.

## Plan détaillé

### 3.1 Intégrer le plugin Tauri notification
- Ajouter à `src-tauri/Cargo.toml` :
  ```toml
  tauri-plugin-notification = "2"
  ```
- Configurer dans `src-tauri/capabilities/default.json`
- Demander la permission au premier lancement

### 3.2 Service de surveillance en arrière-plan
- Créer module `src-tauri/src/reminder_service.rs`
- Timer vérifiant les rappels toutes les 60 secondes
- Logique : `WHERE reminderDate <= now AND isTriggered = false`
- Marquer comme triggered après notification

### 3.3 Fonction de notification
- Titre : "Rappel Beads"
- Corps : "[Issue Title] - [Message ou 'Échéance atteinte']"
- Icône : logo Beads
- Son : notification système par défaut

### 3.4 Action sur clic de notification
- Capturer l'événement de clic
- Ramener l'application au premier plan
- Naviguer vers l'issue concernée (`/issues/{id}`)
- Highlight visuel temporaire

### 3.5 Gestion du démarrage
- Au lancement, vérifier les rappels passés non déclenchés
- Afficher une notification groupée si plusieurs
- Option : "Voir les rappels manqués"

## Critères d'acceptation
- [ ] Plugin notification intégré et configuré
- [ ] Service de surveillance actif en background
- [ ] Notifications déclenchées à l'heure exacte
- [ ] Clic sur notification ouvre l'issue
- [ ] Rappels manqués gérés au démarrage

---

# ÉTAPE 4 : Tests et polish

## Objectif
S'assurer de la robustesse et de l'expérience utilisateur optimale.

## Plan détaillé

### 4.1 Tests unitaires Rust
- Tests pour `reminder_create`, `reminder_list`, etc.
- Tests de la logique de déclenchement
- Tests de persistence (lecture/écriture JSON)
- Mocking du système de fichiers

### 4.2 Tests composants Vue
- Tests du DateTimePicker (validation dates)
- Tests du ReminderDialog (création, annulation)
- Tests de la ReminderList (affichage, actions)

### 4.3 Gestion des cas limites
- Suppression d'une issue → supprimer ses rappels
- Modification d'issue ID (si applicable)
- Fichier reminders.json corrompu → reset avec backup
- Timezone change pendant l'utilisation
- App fermée pendant un rappel prévu

### 4.4 Paramètres utilisateur
- Ajouter section "Notifications" dans Settings
- Toggle : Activer/désactiver les notifications
- Select : Rappel anticipé (Aucun, 5 min, 1h, 1 jour avant)
- Toggle : Son de notification

### 4.5 Accessibilité et UX
- Labels ARIA sur tous les contrôles
- Navigation clavier complète
- Messages d'erreur explicites
- Animations subtiles (apparition/disparition)
- Dark mode support complet

### 4.6 Documentation
- Mettre à jour README.md avec la fonctionnalité
- Ajouter tooltips dans l'UI
- Section FAQ : "Comment configurer les rappels ?"

## Critères d'acceptation
- [ ] Tests unitaires Rust passent
- [ ] Tests composants Vue passent
- [ ] Cas limites documentés et gérés
- [ ] Paramètres utilisateur fonctionnels
- [ ] Accessibilité validée
- [ ] Documentation à jour

---

## Estimation de complexité

| Étape | Complexité | Dépendances |
|-------|------------|-------------|
| 1. Modèle de données | Moyenne | Aucune |
| 2. Interface utilisateur | Haute | Étape 1 |
| 3. Notifications | Haute | Étapes 1, 2 |
| 4. Tests et polish | Moyenne | Étapes 1, 2, 3 |

## Labels suggérés
- feature
- ux
- backend
- notifications

title: Personal Finance Management Application
description:
Develop a comprehensive personal finance management application allowing users to track their expenses, income, and have an overview of their financial situation.

## Objectives
- Allow import of bank statements
- Automatically categorize transactions
- Generate reports and charts
- Define and track budgets
- Multi-account support (checking, savings, etc.)

## Target
Cross-platform desktop application with optional cloud synchronization.

title: Data Model Design
description:
Define the database schema for the application.

## Main Tables
- accounts (id, name, type, balance, currency)
- transactions (id, account_id, amount, date, description, category_id)
- categories (id, name, parent_id, icon, color)
- budgets (id, category_id, amount, period)

## Considerations
- Multi-currency support
- Balance history
- Indexes for frequent queries

title: Dashboard Interface - Overview

Create the main dashboard displaying a summary of the financial situation.

- Monthly expense chart
- Top 5 expense categories
- Budget exceeded alerts
- Recent transactions

## Design

- Rearrangeable widgets
- Light/dark theme

title: Dark mode toggle not persisting after app restart
description:
Users report that the dark mode setting resets to light mode every time the application is restarted.

## Steps to Reproduce
1. Open the app
2. Go to Settings > Appearance
3. Enable dark mode
4. Close and reopen the app
5. Dark mode is disabled

## Expected Behavior
Dark mode preference should persist across sessions.

## Environment
- OS: macOS 14.2, Windows 11
- App version: 1.3.0

title: Memory leak when switching between views rapidly
description:
Application memory usage increases significantly when rapidly switching between different views.

## Symptoms
- RAM usage grows from 200MB to 1.5GB+ over time
- App becomes sluggish after 30+ view switches
- Eventually crashes with out-of-memory error

## Technical Investigation
- Likely cause: event listeners not being cleaned up on component unmount
- Vue devtools shows increasing component count

## Affected Views

title: Fix: detect and repair bd 0.49.4 schema migration errors
description:
## Problème

Après la mise à jour de bd CLI de 0.49.1 vers 0.49.4 via Homebrew, les bases de données existantes échouent à migrer et deviennent inutilisables.

### Erreur rencontrée
```
Error: failed to search issues: sqlite3: SQL logic error: no such column: spec_id
```

### Impact
- Tous les projets avec une base bd existante sont affectés
- L'application affiche une table vide
- Les favoris fonctionnent mais aucune issue n'est chargée

## Solution implémentée

### Backend (Rust)
- Détection de l'erreur "no such column: spec_id" dans execute_bd()
- Nouvelle commande bd_repair_database qui :
  - Vérifie que issues.jsonl existe avant réparation
  - Crée une sauvegarde de beads.db
  - Supprime les fichiers de base corrompus
  - Laisse bd recréer depuis le JSONL

### Frontend
- Composable useRepairDatabase avec repair() et repairAll()
- Helper isSchemaMigrationError() dans bd-api.ts
- Modale de réparation affichée quand l'erreur est détectée

## Issue liée
https://github.com/steveyegge/beads/issues/1516

## Commit
e6a13a4

title: Ajouter une notification quand un favori est supprimé automatiquement
description:
Au démarrage de l'application, la validation automatique des chemins de favoris (useFavorites.ts:23-44) supprime silencieusement les favoris dont le chemin est considéré comme invalide par fsExists(). Aucune notification ni confirmation n'est affichée à l'utilisateur.

Problème constaté : après la mise à jour v1.8.0, un favori pointant vers un chemin existant a disparu de la liste sans avertissement. Le chemin existe bien sur le disque, ce qui suggère un faux négatif transitoire de fsExists() au moment du démarrage (backend Tauri pas encore prêt, timing filesystem, etc.).

La suppression manuelle a bien une modale de confirmation, mais la suppression automatique n'a aucun feedback utilisateur (seulement un console.warn).

Solution proposée :
- Afficher une notification toast quand un favori est retiré automatiquement
- Envisager de ne pas supprimer automatiquement mais marquer le favori comme inaccessible avec une icône d'avertissement
- Ajouter un mécanisme de retry avant suppression pour éviter les faux négatifs transitoires

Fichiers concernés :
- app/composables/useFavorites.ts (lignes 23-44)
- src-tauri/src/lib.rs (fn fs_exists)

title: High CPU and disk usage from constant bd CLI polling
description:
Reported by @pjeby on GitHub issue #2 (comment: https://github.com/w3dev33/beads-task-issue-tracker/issues/2#issuecomment-3865495926).

After fixing the Windows console window popup issue, the app still causes high CPU usage and disk write spikes due to constant background polling of the bd CLI. This is especially noticeable on Windows but also present on macOS (ARM).

## Current Behavior
- The app spawns multiple bd CLI calls every few seconds for data refresh
- This causes significant CPU usage and temperature spikes
- Disk write rates increase noticeably with all the background activity
- The polling continues even when the app window is minimized or covered

## User Suggestions from @pjeby
1. Stop using --no-daemon and avoid running sync constantly
2. Use a change observation method that doesn't require polling (e.g. filesystem watch)
3. At minimum, pause/reduce polling when the window is minimized or not visible

## Investigation Needed
- Profile why CPU usage is higher on Windows compared to macOS ARM
- Measure baseline resource consumption (CPU, disk I/O) with current polling intervals
- Evaluate alternative approaches: filesystem watchers, bd daemon mode, event-driven refresh
- Consider visibility-aware polling (reduce/stop when window is not focused or minimized)

## Related
- GitHub Issue: https://github.com/w3dev33/beads-task-issue-tracker/issues/2
- Original report was about console window popups (fixed), this is the follow-up performance concern

comments.author: w3dev33
comments.text:
Hi @cderv, thanks for the suggestion and for taking the time to describe your use case — really appreciated!

This is now implemented in commit `2ff063b` (will be included in the next release).

You can configure the CLI binary path via **Settings** (Cmd+, on macOS, or the gear icon in the footer):

- Enter `br` (or a full path like `/usr/local/bin/br`) in the CLI Binary field
- Click **Test** to validate the binary responds to `--version`
- Click **Save** to persist the setting

The setting is stored in your app config directory and survives restarts.

**Important note:** The alternative CLI must be fully compatible with the `bd` command interface — same subcommands (`list`, `show`, `create`, `update`, `close`, `sync`, etc.), same flags (`--json`, `--no-daemon`), and same JSON output format. Since `br` from [beads_rust](https://github.com/Dicklesworthstone/beads_rust) is a direct Rust port of `bd` with the same data structure, it should work. If you encounter any issues, check the app's log panel (Cmd+Shift+L) for errors and let me know.

title: Download & Install update fails with no error in logs
description:
When clicking 'Download & Quit' in the update checker dialog, the download fails with an error shown in the UI, but no trace appears in the log file.

Root causes found:
1. Frontend bug: Tauri invoke rejects with a string, not an Error object. The catch block used 'e instanceof Error' which is always false for Tauri errors, so the actual Rust error message was discarded and replaced with a generic 'Failed to download update' message.
2. Backend logging: The download_and_install_update Rust function had no log::error! calls - errors were only returned as Result::Err strings to the frontend, never logged to the file.

Fix:
- Frontend: Handle string errors from Tauri invoke properly (typeof e === 'string')
- Backend: Add log::error! at every failure point (HTTP client, response status, bytes reading, file saving, DMG mounting) with context (URL, status code, byte count)
- Backend: Add log::info! progress traces (filename, request sent, response status, final redirect URL, bytes downloaded, save path)

The actual download failure cause is still unknown - with proper logging it will be captured next time. Possible causes: rustls-tls certificate issue with GitHub CDN, network timeout, or assets not yet available when CI is still building.

title: Detect bd vs br client type for version-aware compatibility
description: Our version-aware compatibility layer (beads-u3t) uses bd version thresholds (< 0.50.0) to decide feature flags like --no-daemon and JSONL usage. But when the user configures 'br' (beads_rust) as the CLI binary, the version number (0.1.x) is from a different product with different semantics. br is frozen at the classic SQLite+JSONL architecture: no daemon, no Dolt, always JSONL. We need to detect which client is in use (bd vs br) and apply the correct feature profile rather than relying solely on version numbers.

title: Test compatibility with bd v0.50.0 (Dolt backend)
description:
bd v0.50.0 removes the daemon, JSONL sync, and SQLite in favor of Dolt (git-for-data). Our app already has version-aware compatibility (supports_daemon_flag, uses_jsonl_files in lib.rs) but needs real-world testing when 0.50.0 ships.

## What to test

- [ ] bd list/show/create/update/close still work via CLI JSON output
- [ ] bd sync works (semantics change from JSONL export to Dolt push/pull)
- [ ] Change detection still works (currently polls issues.jsonl mtime — file will no longer exist)
- [ ] metadata, spec_id, comment_count fields are returned correctly
- [ ] No regressions on existing features (attachments, dependencies, parent-child)

## Likely needed changes

- Change detection mechanism: replace JSONL mtime polling with alternative (poll bd list, or Dolt-native change detection)
- Verify bd sync command output format hasn't changed

## Context

- Compatibility layer added in v1.13.0 (epic beads-awx)
- Version detection: src-tauri/src/lib.rs (supports_daemon_flag, uses_jsonl_files, get_cli_client_info)
- JSONL polling: src-tauri/src/lib.rs (LAST_KNOWN_MTIME, bd_check_updates)

title: Add targeted plan file cleanup to /run-issue skill
description:
## Problem

Claude Code's plan mode stores plan files in the global ~/.claude/plans/ directory. There is no configuration to change this to a project-local directory. After /clear or when starting a new issue, Claude Code sometimes picks up a previous plan file instead of using the newly written one.

This causes confusion when running /run-issue consecutively on different issues.

## Root Cause

- Plan file path is controlled by Claude Code's internal system, not by CLAUDE.md instructions
- The CLAUDE.md instruction to use .claude/plans/ (local) is ignored by the system
- Old plan files accumulate in ~/.claude/plans/ (currently 58 files)
- /clear does not guarantee a fresh plan context

## Solution

Add a targeted cleanup step in the /run-issue skill (before EnterPlanMode) that removes stale plan files older than 24 hours. This avoids deleting plans from other active sessions while cleaning up old ones.

### Implementation

In ~/.claude/commands/run-issue.md, add a new step 6b before 'Enter Plan Mode':

```bash
# Remove plan files older than 24h to prevent stale plan confusion
find ~/.claude/plans/ -name '*.md' -mtime +1 -delete
```

### Why 24h threshold
- Active sessions typically complete within hours
- Plans older than 24h are almost certainly from finished sessions
- This is safe even if multiple Claude Code sessions are running concurrently

title: Display non-blocking dependency types in issue details
description:
The issue detail view only shows two dependency types from the `bd`/`br` JSON output: `blocks` and `parent-child`. All other dependency types are silently dropped during the Rust backend transformation in `lib.rs`.

Both `bd` (Go) and `br` (Rust) support additional non-blocking dependency types that carry useful relationship information. The JSON output from `bd show --json` and `br show --json` already includes these in the `dependencies`/`dependents` arrays with the `dependency_type` field, but the UI never surfaces them.

## Missing types common to both CLIs

| Type | Category | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| `discovered-from` | Association | Issue found while working on another |
| `relates-to` | Graph link | Loose knowledge graph edge |
| `related` | Association | Bidirectional see-also link |
| `duplicates` | Graph link | Deduplication link |
| `supersedes` | Graph link | Version chain link |
| `caused-by` | Reference | Triggered by target (audit trail) |

## Additional types in `bd` only (not yet in `br`)

`tracks`, `until`, `validates`, `authored-by`, `assigned-to`, `approved-by`, `attests`, `delegated-from`

## Where the gap is

The backend `BdRawDependency` struct already deserializes the `dependency_type` field correctly. The drop happens in the transformation from `BdRawIssue` to the frontend `Issue` struct, which only filters for `"blocks"` and `"parent-child"`.

## Suggestion

We could add a new section in the issue detail view (alongside the existing Parents, Children, and Dependencies sections) to display these non-blocking relationships grouped by type. Starting with types common to both CLIs would cover the most useful cases.

title: Soft-deleted issues are seen as open (bd 0.49)
description:
Created a new bead, opened the detail, pressed delete, ok, interface glitches a bit, the issue is still there as open.

In the .jsonl the issue has `tombstone` as status but the UI shows it as "Open".

https://github.com/w3dev33/beads-task-issue-tracker/blob/e57702444be042e83c9e298841f2961b0b496a33/app/types/issue.ts#L2

The [possible statuses](https://github.com/steveyegge/beads/blob/ab3e940e7f291a8c32a4b9fce7279095fc87cf03/internal/types/types.go#L462-L471) in bd (at the time of writing) are:
```
	StatusOpen       Status = "open"
	StatusInProgress Status = "in_progress"
	StatusBlocked    Status = "blocked"
	StatusDeferred   Status = "deferred" // Deliberately put on ice for later
	StatusClosed     Status = "closed"
	StatusTombstone  Status = "tombstone" // Soft-deleted issue
	StatusPinned     Status = "pinned"    // Persistent bead that stays open indefinitely
	StatusHooked     Status = "hooked"    // Work attached to an agent's hook (GUPP)
```

comments.author: w3dev33
comments.text:
Fixed in [v1.17.2](https://github.com/w3dev33/beads-task-issue-tracker/releases/tag/v1.17.2).

The app now supports all `bd` statuses (`deferred`, `tombstone`, `pinned`, `hooked`). Previously, `normalize_issue_status()` in the Rust backend converted any unknown status to `"open"` — that's why tombstone issues were showing up as Open.

**What changed:**
- Tombstone issues are filtered out of the default view and excluded from dashboard stats
- Each new status has its own distinct badge color (amber for deferred, stone for deleted, purple for pinned, cyan for hooked)
- All statuses are available in the filter dropdowns

Thank you for discovering and reporting this issue, @danielefranceschi! The detailed report with the link to bd's status definitions was really helpful and made this a quick fix. Much appreciated! 🙏

title: fix: blue flash animation triggers on all rows when switching projects
description:
When switching between projects, all issue rows display the blue flash animation meant for newly added issues. The flash should only trigger for genuinely new issues detected during polling, not when loading a different project entirely.

## Root cause
The new-issue detection compares current issue IDs against a previous set. When the project changes, the previous set belongs to the old project, so every issue in the new project appears 'new'.

## Expected behavior
- Switching projects: no flash on any row (full reload)
- New issue detected during polling in same project: flash on that row only

## Related
- Commit 69395e2: feat: highlight newly added issues with blue flash animation

title: Adapt to bd server-only mode (embedded Dolt removed)
description:
## Context

Suite à notre issue #1935 sur steveyegge/beads (SIGSEGV on concurrent access + orphaned lock files), Steve Yegge a **supprimé entièrement le mode Dolt embarqué** de Beads. Beads fonctionne désormais uniquement en **mode serveur**.

Commits référencés :
- `53e9f8cc` — suppression du mode embarqué
- `1ade634a` — suppression de la dépendance `dolthub/driver`
- `9abb7dd5` — nettoyage des artefacts restants

## Résumé du fil de l'issue #1935

- **Notre report** : SIGSEGV (crash nil pointer) quand 2 commandes `bd` tournent en parallèle + lock files orphelins
- **Confirmation par toddmedema** : même crash depuis un orchestrateur avec appels concurrents
- **Réponse de Steve** : le mode embarqué est supprimé, mode serveur uniquement. Le SIGSEGV ne peut plus se produire en mode serveur.

## Précisions : ce que "mode serveur" signifie concrètement

**Il n'y a PAS de base de données globale type PostgreSQL à installer.**

### Avant (mode embarqué - supprimé)
Chaque appel `bd list`, `bd show`, etc. lançait **son propre moteur Dolt en mémoire** dans le process. Deux appels simultanés = deux moteurs sur les mêmes fichiers → crash SIGSEGV.

### Maintenant (mode serveur/daemon)
Un **daemon `bd` local** tourne en arrière-plan **par projet**. Il :
- Gère l'accès à la base Dolt (dans `.beads/dolt/`)
- Reçoit les commandes CLI via RPC
- Sérialise les accès → plus de crash concurrent

C'est **local au projet**, pas global à la machine. Chaque repo a son `.beads/dolt/` avec ses données et son daemon. Le daemon se lance automatiquement (`auto-start-daemon: true` dans config.yaml).

### Impact réel pour nous
- **Rien de dramatique** — pas besoin d'installer un serveur de BDD séparé
- Le daemon est **déjà utilisé** dans notre version actuelle (bd 0.55.4)
- Le changement : le **fallback embarqué** n'existera plus si le daemon ne tourne pas
- On devra potentiellement vérifier que le daemon est démarré avant nos appels `bd`, mais `auto-start-daemon` est activé par défaut → devrait être transparent
- Notre workaround de sérialisation des appels `bd` reste pertinent par prudence

## Actions à prendre

1. Tester avec la prochaine version de `bd` sans mode embarqué
2. Vérifier que le daemon auto-start fonctionne bien depuis notre app Tauri
3. Éventuellement ajouter un healthcheck daemon dans notre app

## Références

- https://github.com/steveyegge/beads/issues/1935
- https://github.com/steveyegge/beads/issues/1516

title: Add beads-probe client mode to Beads
description: Add external data source mode to Beads Task-Issue Tracker. Allow the app to consume data from an external HTTP endpoint instead of direct bd CLI calls. UI: New 'Data Source' section in Settings dialog with: - Radio toggle: 'Direct (bd CLI)' (default) vs 'External endpoint' - URL input field (default http://localhost:9100) visible when External is selected - Connection status indicator (connected/disconnected) showing if the endpoint responds - Setting stored in localStorage per project (same pattern as other per-project settings). Backend (Rust): - Read the data source setting from frontend config - When 'External endpoint' is selected, fetch data via HTTP GET from the configured URL instead of spawning bd CLI processes - When 'Direct' is selected, current behavior unchanged (bd CLI calls) - Simple conditional: if config.use_external { fetch_from_http(url) } else { fetch_from_bd_cli() } - The external endpoint URL is generic (no reference to any specific monitoring product). PHASE 1 PURPOSE: This is the first client of beads-probe (beads-manager-cz3). Using Beads in External mode validates the probe's data format, refresh, and API correctness before any external monitoring integration. Both modes must produce identical results in the UI. See .claude/comparaison-beadbox.md for full architectural details.

title: Centralized monitoring — real-time multi-project multi-user dashboard
description:
Read-only monitoring layer that aggregates and displays issue state across multiple projects and users in real-time.

This is the foundation layer — it works standalone without the orchestrator. Users can work however they want (CLI, IDE, manually) and the monitoring dashboard shows where everyone is.

Core features:
- Aggregate .beads/ state from multiple projects (local or network)
- Display real-time progress per project, per role, per user/agent
- Dependency graph visualization — what is blocked, what is ready
- File watcher on each project's .beads/ for instant updates
- No write operations — pure observation

Data sources:
- .beads/issues.jsonl from each project (existing)
- ~/.beads/orchestrator-events.jsonl (when orchestrator is active)
- beads-probe metrics endpoint (existing probe-adapter.ts)

Builds on existing work:
- probe-adapter.ts already normalizes multi-project data
- useDashboard.ts already aggregates stats
- PathSelector + FolderPicker already handle multi-project navigation
- File watcher (useChangeDetection) already provides instant updates

This is the read-only foundation. The orchestrator is an optional add-on that adds write capabilities (task distribution, worktree management, merge).

title: Hook-based orchestration — Claude Code hooks as event bridge for agent monitoring
description:
Use Claude Code hooks system to bridge agent activity to the Beads Task-Issue Tracker app in real-time, without polling or server infrastructure.

Architecture:
- SessionStart hook: injects task context (assigned issue, worktree path) into the agent session
- PostToolUse hook (Bash matcher): intercepts bd mutations (create, close, update, delete) and git commits, writes events to a shared JSONL file
- Stop hook: signals task completion to the orchestrator, triggers worktree merge
- SessionEnd hook: cleanup worktree, archive transcript

Event flow:
  Agent runs bd command → PostToolUse hook fires → writes to ~/.beads/orchestrator-events.jsonl → file watcher detects change → app updates UI in real-time

Key advantages:
- Zero infrastructure (no server, no database, no polling)
- Native integration with Claude Code (hooks are a first-class feature)
- File watcher compatible (JSONL append = instant detection)
- Works with any agent that supports hooks (not just Claude Code)
- 19 hook events available for fine-grained monitoring

Compact resilience:
- PostToolUse updates .claude/agent-context.json with current state after significant actions
- SessionStart hook (matcher: resume|compact) re-injects context on resume
- Agent is self-healing — context persisted on disk, never lost on compact

Issue context propagation:
- Orchestrator writes .claude/assigned-issue marker file before launching agent
- All hooks read this file to tag events with the correct issue ID
- cwd (worktree path) provides secondary identification

title: Worktree-based agent orchestrator — lightweight alternative to Gastown
description:
Design a lightweight orchestrator that distributes beads/issues to isolated agents using git worktrees, without requiring Dolt server mode or Gastown.

Architecture:
- Central base: git repo (main branch) with .beads/ JSONL as source of truth
- Orchestrator loop: scan dependency graph → identify unblocked tasks → spawn agent per task in a worktree → merge on completion → repeat
- Each agent works in isolation (git worktree add), has its own .beads/, its own code copy
- On task completion: merge worktree into main, mark task as done, cleanup worktree
- Newly unblocked tasks become eligible for next cycle
- Beads Task-Issue Tracker app monitors main branch via native file watcher — instant visibility

Key principles:
- A bead/issue is the atomic unit of work — one agent per task, no concurrent writes
- Dependencies are sequential by nature — no need for shared database or server
- JSONL is append-only — git merge is trivial, near-zero conflicts
- No Dolt, no server, no polling — just git + worktrees + file watcher
- The app becomes the visual control plane, the orchestrator is embedded in the Rust/Tauri backend

Role-based distribution:
- Tasks tagged by role (backend, frontend, test)
- Orchestrator assigns to available agent/human matching the role
- Humans get notifications, AI agents get worktrees
- Smart Kanban: knows WHEN a task can start and WHO can take it

Existing solutions in the same space:
- Composio Agent Orchestrator (github.com/ComposioHQ/agent-orchestrator)
- ccswarm (github.com/nwiizo/ccswarm)
- Ralph TUI (ralph-tui.com)
- Intent by Augment Code
- Claude Flow (github.com/ruvnet/claude-flow)

Our differentiator: native desktop visual control plane with real-time file watcher monitoring.

title: Display dependency relationships between issues in the issue table
description:
Currently the issue table shows epics with their children (parent-child hierarchy), but dependencies between issues (blocked-by / blocks) are not visible in the table view. Users have to open each issue to see its dependencies.

Requested enhancement:
- Show dependency indicators directly in the issue table rows
- Visualize which issues are blocked and by what
- Visualize which issues are ready to start (all dependencies resolved)
- Within an epic's children, show the dependency chain between siblings

Possible approaches:
1. Dependency column with icons/badges showing blocked-by count and blocking count
2. Color coding or opacity for blocked issues (greyed out until unblocked)
3. Small inline dependency arrows between sibling rows within an epic group
4. Tooltip or expandable section showing the dependency list on hover/click
5. Status indicator: 'ready' (all deps resolved) vs 'blocked' (waiting on N issues)

This is especially important for the orchestration epic (beads-manager-l1m9) where task distribution depends on understanding the dependency graph at a glance.

Example: within an epic, if child .3 is blocked by .1 and .2, the table should make this visible without opening each issue.

title: Support nested sub-tasks in issue table (recursive parent-child hierarchy)
description:
Currently the issue table only supports 2 levels: epic → children. Sub-tasks (children of children) appear as orphans in the table.

Example: beads-manager-l1m9 (epic) → beads-manager-l1m9.1 (child) → beads-manager-l1m9.1.1 (sub-task)
The sub-task l1m9.1.1 is not grouped under l1m9.1 because groupIssues() only looks for parents with type 'epic'.

Important constraint: sub-tasks can ONLY exist under children of an epic. The dot notation hierarchy is strict:
  prefix-abc        (epic — always the root)
  prefix-abc.1      (child of epic)
  prefix-abc.1.1    (sub-task — child of child, always under an epic descendant)

There are no floating sub-tasks — the root is always an epic. This simplifies the implementation: no need for a fully recursive generic container system. We just need to support 3 levels within the existing epic grouping.

Needed changes in app/utils/issue-helpers.ts:
- groupIssues() should detect sub-tasks (IDs with 2+ dots like abc.1.1) and nest them under their parent child (abc.1)
- IssueTable should render 3 levels of indentation: epic → child → sub-task
- Progress bars should cascade: sub-task progress rolls up into parent child, then into epic
- Collapse/expand should work at each level (epic collapses all, child collapses its sub-tasks)

bd supports this via dot notation and --parent flag:
  bd create --parent prefix-abc 'child'           → prefix-abc.1
  bd create --parent prefix-abc.1 'sub-task'      → prefix-abc.1.1

title: Auto-detect CLI client at startup: prefer br over bd
description:
## Problem

The app defaults to `bd` (hardcoded in CLI_BINARY static). Users who have `br` installed must manually switch in Settings. If only `br` is available, the app fails on startup trying to run `bd`.

## Solution

At startup (around line 4243 in lib.rs), implement auto-detection:

1. If `config.cli_binary` is explicitly set by user → respect that choice
2. Otherwise, probe in order: `br` first, then `bd` as fallback
3. If neither is found → error

Detection = run `<binary> --version` from /tmp (already done for the configured binary).

## Implementation Notes

- Change the default in `CLI_BINARY` from `"bd"` to be determined at runtime
- Add a `detect_preferred_client()` function that tries `br --version` then `bd --version`
- Only auto-detect when `config.cli_binary` is empty or unset (user override takes precedence)
- Log which client was auto-selected and why
- Update SettingsDialog to show which client was auto-detected vs manually chosen

## Acceptance Criteria

- [x] App starts correctly with only `br` installed (no `bd`)
- [x] App starts correctly with only `bd` installed (no `br`)  
- [x] App starts correctly with both installed → picks `br`
- [x] User can still override in Settings, override persists across restarts
- [x] Startup logs clearly show detection result

Dolt commit hash polling for server-mode change detection
description:
## Context

In bd 0.56+ server mode, our file watcher cannot detect Dolt data changes (data lives in MySQL memory, not on disk). Current workaround: bd_check_changed() always returns true, forcing full poll every cycle — wasteful and generates false positives.

beatbox solved this by polling Dolt commit hashes instead of file mtimes. We should adopt the same approach.

## Design

### SQL Query

SELECT DOLT_HASHOF('HEAD') — returns a 40-char SHA. ~2-5ms on localhost. Compare to cached previous hash: if different, data changed.

### Rust Backend (lib.rs)

- New LAST_KNOWN_COMMIT_HASH cache (LazyLock Mutex HashMap)
- New Tauri command bd_check_dolt_changed(cwd):
  1. Only active when requires_dolt_server_mode() and project_uses_dolt()
  2. Query Dolt server via mysql on localhost
  3. Compare hash to cached value, return true if changed
  4. On connection error, fall back to mtime polling
- New command bd_reset_dolt_hash(cwd) for project switch cleanup

### TypeScript Frontend (bd-api.ts)

- bdCheckDoltChanged(path?) wrapper with fallback to bdCheckChanged()
- bdResetDoltHash(path?) for cleanup

### Adaptive Polling (useAdaptivePolling.ts)

- Detect server mode at mount
- Use bdCheckDoltChanged for cheap check in server mode
- Keep mtime for embedded mode (bd 0.49 / br)

## Acceptance Criteria

- bd_check_dolt_changed() returns false when no commits occurred
- Returns true after a bd create / bd close / bd edit
- Falls back to mtime if Dolt server unreachable
- No regression on bd 0.49 embedded mode
- Port derived from Steve path-hash approach (13307-14307 range)

## References

- beatbox approach: Dolt commit hash polling
- Parent issue: beads-task-issue-tracker-8lz
- Branch: feat/bd-056-server-mode

title: CLI binary tracker (src/cli/main.rs)
description: Create standalone CLI binary using the same tracker::Engine crate. Commands: create, list, show, update, close, delete, search, comments add, dep add/remove, label add/remove. JSON output with --json flag. Bundled in app package (macOS .app, Linux /usr/bin, Windows Program Files). This is the AI agent interface — must be usable without the Tauri app running.

title: Task state machine (tracker/workflow.rs)
description: Define allowed status transitions with configurable rules. Default workflow: open → in_progress → blocked → closed, open → deferred, blocked → in_progress, any → closed. Rules stored in config.yaml per project. Engine enforces transitions: reject invalid ones with clear error. Support custom statuses beyond the defaults. Emit events on transition (foundation for automation triggers in .20).

title: Dependency-aware scheduling (tracker/scheduler.rs)
description: Implement a scheduler that analyzes the dependency graph and determines which tasks are actionable. A task is 'ready' when: status=open, all blockers are closed, all parent tasks are open/in_progress. Expose as ready() query (already in Engine API). Add topological sort for execution order. Detect circular dependencies and flag them. This is the brain for agent orchestration — it answers 'what can be worked on next?'.

title: Agent distribution via worktrees (tracker/orchestrator.rs)
description: Link to epic beads-l1m9 (worktree-based orchestrator). Implement the orchestration loop: 1) call scheduler to get ready tasks, 2) for each ready task, create a git worktree, 3) assign task to agent (AI or human), 4) monitor completion, 5) merge worktree back to main, 6) mark task closed, 7) re-evaluate ready tasks. Each worktree gets its own .tracker/ DB copy. JSONL merge on worktree completion. Tauri UI shows live status of all agents/worktrees.

title: Multi-project dashboard
description: Support monitoring multiple projects simultaneously. Each project has its own .tracker/ folder and DB. Dashboard view: list of projects with issue counts, status summary, last activity. Quick switch between projects. Cross-project search. Aggregate KPIs (total open issues across all projects). Store project list in app-level config (~/.config/com.beads.manager/projects.json).

title: Tombstone issues visible in epic children list
description: Issues with status=tombstone (hard-deleted via br delete --force --hard) still appear in the epic's children list in the UI. They should be filtered out. Reproduction: delete a child issue with br delete --force --hard, the issue gets status=tombstone but still shows in the parent epic's expanded row. Expected: tombstone issues are excluded from the children list, same as closed issues can be filtered. Found while working on epic beads-qcud.

title: Tombstone issues visible in epic children list
description: Issues with status=tombstone (hard-deleted via br delete --force --hard) still appear in the epic's children list in the UI. They should be filtered out. The built-in engine is accessible via two entry points: Tauri app (direct rusqlite, fast) and a CLI binary 'tracker' bundled in the app for AI agents (Claude Code, worktree agents, orchestrators). title: ID generation (tracker/ids.rs)
description: Parent: beads-qcud (Phase 1). Depends on: beads-t9qc. Implement ID generation: prefix-base36 format (e.g. tracker-a4f2). Read prefix from config.yaml. 4-char random base36 string (1.6M combinations). Collision check against DB before returning. Must be compatible with existing bd/br IDs for future migration.

title: Issue CRUD operations (tracker/issues.rs)

Implement full issue CRUD: list (open/closed/all), show (by ID with comments+deps+labels inlined), create (with all fields), update (partial, only provided fields), close (set status+closed_at), delete (soft or hard). Relation types: blocks, relates-to, duplicates, similar, references. Update issue updated_at on every mutation.

title: Full-text search with FTS5 (tracker/search.rs)
description: Parent: beads-qcud (Phase 1). Depends on: issues.rs. Create FTS5 virtual table indexing title+description+notes. Implement search(query) returning ranked results. Keep FTS index in sync via triggers on INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE. Must handle special characters and partial matches.

title: Import .beads issues into .tracker on backend switch
description:
When switching to Built-in backend in Settings, the .tracker/ database is empty — all existing issues from .beads/ disappear. Need an import mechanism that reads issues from br/bd (via JSON output) and creates them in the tracker SQLite engine.

## Approach
1. When user switches to Built-in and .tracker/tracker.db is empty (or new), detect that .beads/ exists with issues
2. Prompt user: Import existing issues from .beads/?
3. Run br list -s all --json to get all issues
4. For each issue, run br show <id> --json to get full details (comments, labels, deps)
5. Create each issue in tracker Engine preserving: ID, title, body, status, type, priority, assignee, labels, comments, dependencies, parent, timestamps
6. Show progress and summary when done

## Notes
- Should preserve original IDs if possible (or create a mapping)
- Comments need author + timestamp preservation
- Dependencies (blocks/blocked-by) must be re-created after all issues exist
- Attachments in .beads/attachments/ should be copied/symlinked
- Could also be a standalone CLI command: beads-tracker import-beads

title: Add cargo install support for beads-tracker CLI binary
description:
The beads-tracker CLI binary is currently only available via cargo build from the source tree (target/debug/beads-tracker). End users and AI agents need a simple way to install it.

## Goal
Allow installing beads-tracker globally via:
```bash
cargo install --git https://github.com/w3dev33/beads-task-issue-tracker --bin beads-tracker
```

## Tasks
- Ensure the crate metadata in Cargo.toml supports cargo install (publish-ready package structure)
- Document the install command in README and tracker-cli.md
- Consider adding a Homebrew formula or GitHub release binary later

title: Keyboard shortcut Cmd+R for data refresh
description: Add Cmd+R (macOS) / Ctrl+R (Windows) keyboard shortcut to trigger a full data refresh (same as the existing reload button). This mirrors the browser refresh habit but stays in-app.

title: Parse config.yaml in tracker ProjectConfig::load()
description: ProjectConfig::load() in src-tauri/src/tracker/config.rs always returns defaults (TODO at line 27). It should parse .tracker/config.yaml when present to read issue_prefix, folder_name, and actor. This is needed so migrated projects keep their original ID prefix (e.g. bd-, kybio-) instead of falling back to 'tracker'.

Inclure blockedBy/blocks dans list_issues() pour réactivité temps réel
description:
Dans le built-in tracker (feat/builtin-tracker), list_issues() retourne blocked_by et blocks vides — ces champs ne sont peuplés que via get_issue() (bdShow au clic). Résultat : l'icône de verrou n'est pas réactive au polling.

Fix : ajouter fetch_all_block_relations() dans tracker/issues.rs qui lit toutes les dépendances en une seule requête SQL, puis distribue les blocked_by/blocks à chaque issue dans list_issues(). Cela rend le polling complet et élimine le cache stale côté frontend.

Bonus : avec des données toujours fraîches, on peut aussi envisager un statut visuel automatique basé sur l'état bloqué (icône ou badge) sans dépendre d'un bdShow préalable.

title: Bundle beads-tracker CLI as Tauri sidecar
description:
Le binaire CLI beads-tracker (src-tauri/src/bin/tracker.rs) est indispensable pour que les agents IA (Claude Code, etc.) puissent interagir avec le backend .tracker/ depuis le terminal. Sans lui, l'IA ne peut pas créer, fermer, ou lister des issues quand le projet utilise le mode tracker built-in.

Actuellement le CLI ne peut être installé que via cargo install, ce qui nécessite un toolchain Rust. Il doit être bundlé avec l'app Tauri.

Plan :
1. Configurer le sidecar Tauri (bundle.externalBin dans tauri.conf.json)
2. Build script pour compiler beads-tracker avec le bon target triple
3. GitHub Actions : compiler + attacher le binaire standalone aux releases
4. Premier lancement : détecter si le CLI est dans le PATH, proposer un symlink /usr/local/bin/beads-tracker
5. Section Settings : statut CLI installé / bouton Install CLI
6. Mettre à jour beads-cli.md pour pointer vers beads-tracker quand le mode tracker est actif

Fichiers concernés : tauri.conf.json, src-tauri/build.rs, .github/workflows/release.yml, SettingsDialog.vue, useBackendMode.ts, lib.rs

Critères d'acceptation :
- Installer l'app = binaire beads-tracker dans le bundle
- L'utilisateur peut installer le CLI dans le PATH depuis Settings ou au premier lancement
- beads-tracker list fonctionne depuis le terminal
- Les releases GitHub incluent le binaire standalone en téléchargement séparé
- Les agents IA utilisent beads-tracker comme ils utilisent br

title: Test notification light theme
bd 0.59.0 breaks issue loading: `bd list --json` returns plain text instead of JSON
description:
## Summary

The viewer shows no issues when using `bd 0.59.0`. The root cause is a regression in `bd 0.59.0` where `bd list --json` ignores the `--json` flag and outputs human-readable text instead of JSON.

The viewer's `parse_issues_tolerant()` function (in `lib.rs:642`) receives non-JSON text and fails to parse it, resulting in an empty issue list displayed to the user.

## Upstream Bug

This is tracked in the beads repo: https://github.com/steveyegge/beads/issues/2395

**Regression:** `bd 0.58.0` outputs valid JSON; `bd 0.59.0` does not.

## Impact

- Viewer shows **zero issues** despite having 500+ issues in the project
- All `bd list`/`bd ready`/`bd_poll_data` calls silently fail
- Logs show only startup + watcher events, no `bd_list` calls after startup (the viewer may short-circuit before calling `bd list` due to earlier parse failures)

## Workaround Attempts

- **Downgrading to bd 0.58.0:** Not viable — the Dolt database created by 0.59.0 is incompatible with 0.58.0 (bd hangs on startup trying to connect to the Dolt server)
- The only fix is either:
  1. A new `bd` release that fixes `--json` output
  2. A viewer workaround (e.g., using `bd export` as fallback, or `bd show --json` which still works)

## Environment

- macOS ARM64
- bd version 0.59.0 (Homebrew)
- Viewer version 1.24.2
- Project has Dolt backend with 500+ issues

## Reproduction

1. Install `bd 0.59.0` via Homebrew
2. Open a project with Dolt backend
3. Viewer shows empty/no issues
4. Check logs: no `bd_list` entries, only watcher events

## Suggested Fix

The viewer could detect the broken `--json` output (e.g., if the response doesn't start with `[` or `{`) and fall back to `bd export` or `bd show --json` for individual issues.

comments.author: Maxpceo
w3dev33
comments.text:
Thanks for the detailed report @Maxpceo.

This is a confirmed upstream regression in `bd 0.59.0` — tracked in [steveyegge/beads#2395](https://github.com/steveyegge/beads/issues/2395). The fix belongs upstream, not in the viewer — we can't maintain workarounds for every upstream CLI regression, as it would mask real bugs that should be fixed at the source.

The good news is you already have everything you need to recover. You mentioned your `issues.jsonl` has 544 valid entries — that's your full backup. You can stay on `bd 0.59.0` and simply rebuild your database by re-importing from that file.

I'd suggest asking your AI assistant (Claude, Copilot, etc.) to help you write a migration script that reads your `issues.jsonl` and re-creates each issue via `bd create` in a fresh project. It's straightforward — all your data is already there in a clean format.

Hope this helps — don't hesitate to come back if we misunderstood the issue.

comments.author: Maxpceo
comments.text: @w3dev33 Thank you very much, I really like your app. Please don't abandon it

title: The task lock icon does not disappear even after the task is unlocked.
description: I created an epic where all but one of the tasks were blocked. It was necessary to complete this task to unlock all the others. After completing the task that was supposed to unlock the others, the lock icon does not disappear.

comments.author: w3dev33
comments.text:
Thanks for the detailed report — this is a confirmed bug.

**What was happening:** when you completed the blocking task, the lock icon on dependent issues was not disappearing because the app was caching dependency data from a previous detail view and never invalidating it when the blocker's status changed.

**Fix included in next release:**
- The lock icon now disappears automatically when the polling detects that the blocker has been closed
- Additionally, when you close a blocking issue from within the app, dependent issues are immediately refreshed

**Known limitation:** the lock icon will only appear on a blocked issue after you open its detail panel at least once. Showing it upfront for all issues would require fetching each issue individually — too costly to do on every poll cycle.

title: [Bug] Unable to remove projects from Favorites sidebar / Duplicate entries persisting
description:
## Description:
I am unable to remove repositories from the "FAVORITES" sidebar in the macOS desktop app.

## Environment:
*   **App Version:** v1.20.0
*   **OS:** macOS Tahoe 26.4 Beta 1
*   **Backend:** Dolt (Migrated from SQLite via the v0.50+ migration process)
*   **Architecture:** CGO-enabled binary (installed via Homebrew)

## Steps to Reproduce:
1.  Open Beads Task-Issue Tracker (v1.20.0).
2.  Locate the "FAVORITES" section in the left sidebar.
3.  Attempt to remove a project by clicking on the cross next to the name of the project on the lest sidebar.
4.  Observe that nothing happens, the removal action fails to persist after a reload.

## Observed Behavior:
*   Projects cannot be removed from the list.
*   The state persists across app restarts.

## Expected Behavior:
*   Users should be able to un-favorite/remove a project from the sidebar via a context menu or icon.
*   The list should enforce uniqueness by project path/ID to prevent duplicates.

comments.author: w3dev33
comments.text:
Thanks for reporting this @leonardsellem!

This has been fixed and will be included in the next release. Appreciate the detailed bug report!

title: Constantly opens console windows running bd - like multiple times/second
description: Windows user here - just tried this and from the moment I added a project for the first time it started spamming console windows shelling out to bd.  I couldn't use it because the popups were all stealing focus and jumping in front of the app.  Removing the project from favorites stopped the windows from popping up, but opening a project again resumed the popups.  The debug logs didn't show anything at all, even with verbose off.

comments.author: w3dev33
comments.text:
Hi @pjeby, thanks for reporting this!

We've identified the root cause. This is a Windows-specific issue: on Windows, each background CLI call to `bd` opens a visible console window by default, which steals focus. Our app refreshes data every few seconds with multiple `bd` calls, so that results in a constant stream of console popups.

On macOS/Linux subprocesses are invisible, which is why we didn't catch this during development.

The fix is straightforward and will be included in the next release. Thanks again for taking the time to report this — really appreciate it! 🙏

comments.author: pjeby
comments.text: Thanks, that fixed the focusing issue!  Now though, the CPU usage and temperature is spiking with the tracker running; I assume because it's still spawning lots of stuff in the background?  Maybe in a future release it would be possible to get it to use a method of observing changes that doesn't require polling like that, e.g. not using `--no-daemon` and not running sync constantly.  Even just having it not do that stuff when the window is minimized or covered by other windows would be helpful, since I could leave it running in the background without the CPU and disk churn that it's doing.  (My disk write rates jump up with all the activity as well.)

comments.author: w3dev33
comments.text:
Hi @pjeby, great feedback — we just shipped v1.10.0 which addresses the CPU/disk usage issue you described.

The root cause was that every 5-second poll cycle spawned 6+ bd CLI processes (3 syncs + 3 list/ready calls), totaling 72+ process spawns per minute — even when nothing had changed. On Windows where process creation is expensive, this causes exactly the CPU spike and disk churn you observed.

v1.10.0 implements a 4-layer optimization:

1. **Sync cooldown** — The Rust backend now skips redundant `bd sync` calls within a 10-second window (was running 3+ times per cycle)
2. **Filesystem mtime check** — Before any polling, we now `stat()` the `.beads/beads.db` file. If the modification time hasn't changed, the entire poll cycle is skipped — zero bd processes spawned
3. **Batched poll command** — When changes are detected, a single backend call now fetches all data (open + closed + ready issues) instead of 3 separate IPC calls
4. **Adaptive polling intervals** — The fixed 5s interval is replaced by context-aware timing:
   - Active + focused: 5s (same as before)
   - Window blurred (on another monitor): 30s
   - Idle (2min no interaction): 60s
   - Minimized/hidden: completely paused (resumes immediately when you bring it back)

In practice, since the database only changes when someone explicitly runs a `bd` command, the mtime check returns "no changes" on virtually every poll cycle. This means ~0 process spawns per minute during normal use, even when the app is focused and active.

Regarding your suggestion about dropping `--no-daemon` and using the bd daemon instead — we considered it, but the daemon approach would actually add overhead in our case. The bd daemon is designed for CLI users: it runs as a separate long-running process that watches for file changes and auto-syncs. In a Tauri desktop app, that means managing an additional background process lifecycle (start it when a project is selected, stop it on switch, handle crashes, etc.), plus IPC between the app and the daemon. With the mtime check (Layer 2), we've achieved the same zero-cost change detection as a file watcher — a single `stat()` syscall — but without the complexity of a separate daemon process. The `--no-daemon` flag actually prevents bd from spawning its own background process on each CLI call, which would have been even worse for resource usage (dozens of orphan daemon processes).

**Troubleshooting with the Debug Panel:**
If you want to verify the improvement or track down any remaining issues, you can use the built-in Debug Panel:

1. Open it from the footer bar (bug icon at the bottom of the app)
2. Logging is automatically enabled while the panel is open — no extra setup needed
3. Click **Verbose ON** to see detailed polling activity, including:
   - `[bd_check_changed] mtime unchanged` — the mtime guard skipped the poll (zero bd processes spawned)
   - `[bd_check_changed] mtime changed` — a change was detected, triggering a batched poll
   - `[bd_poll_data] Batched poll done` — the single batched call that replaces the old 3-call cycle
   - `[sync] Skipping sync (cooldown)` — a redundant sync was skipped
4. The log view supports **scrolling up** to inspect older entries without being pushed back to the bottom by live updates — click the **Bottom** button to resume auto-scroll when done

In normal use, you should see mostly `mtime unchanged` entries, confirming that no bd processes are being spawned.

You can grab the release here: https://github.com/w3dev33/beads-task-issue-tracker/releases/tag/v1.10.0

Would love to hear if this resolves the CPU/disk usage on your end!

title: UI: Distinguish resolved vs active blockers in dependency section
description:
## Problem

When a task has a "BLOCKED BY" dependency and the blocking task is already **CLOSED**, the UI still renders it identically to an active (unresolved) blocker. This is confusing because it implies the task is still blocked when it is not.

The data model is correct — `bd blocked` already computes the right result (resolved blockers are not counted). The issue is purely presentational.

**Example:** Task `jox.7` (IN PROGRESS) shows "BLOCKED BY `jox.6`", but `jox.6` is CLOSED. The dashboard counter correctly shows `BLOCKED: 0`, yet the detail panel gives no visual distinction.

## Proposed behavior

- **Active blocker** (open / in_progress): show as-is with current "BLOCKED BY" styling
- **Resolved blocker** (closed / wontdo): visually distinguish — e.g., strikethrough, greyed-out text, or a "resolved" badge

## Why not handle this at the workflow level?

We considered mutating dependency types (e.g., changing `blocks` → `deps` when a task moves to IN PROGRESS), but this loses the historical semantics of the blocking relationship. The fix belongs in the presentation layer, not the data layer.

## Acceptance criteria

1. A "BLOCKED BY" dependency where the blocker is CLOSED renders visually distinct from one where the blocker is OPEN
2. The dependency record itself is unchanged — no data mutation occurs
3. The BLOCKED counter in the dashboard sidebar continues to show only actively-blocked tasks (existing behavior, no change needed)

description:

But its created on  Apple and looking for logs inside **/home/<user>/Library/Logs/com.beads.manager/beads.log** which would be an Apple OS 

Also, although its detecting my beads projects, its not finding issues, and I can't see what is going on because of lack of logs. I can only guess without looking at the source code, that its having issues running the bd binary, perhaps you hardcoded the path?

```

```


comments.author: w3dev33
comments.text:
GitHub issue #15 response (draft):

Hi @ubuntupunk — thanks for reporting this, and glad you like the interface!

You're absolutely right: the log path was hardcoded to macOS's ~/Library/Logs/ convention. Since we develop exclusively on macOS, cross-platform issues like this tend to slip through — especially when there's little feedback from users on other platforms. So reports like yours are genuinely valuable.

**The fix:** We've updated get_log_path() to use platform-aware paths — on Linux it now resolves to ~/.local/share/com.beads.manager/logs/beads.log (following XDG conventions), which matches where the Tauri log plugin actually writes. We also fixed get_extended_path() to use the correct PATH separator and environment variables on Windows.

Regarding the bd binary path: the app already includes ~/.local/bin in its search path, so your bd installation should be found. The real issue was the chicken-and-egg problem — no logs meant no way to diagnose what was happening.

The fix is in release **v1.24.3** (https://github.com/w3dev33/beads-task-issue-tracker/releases/tag/v1.24.3).

**Some context on this project:** Beads Task Issue Tracker was born out of my personal learning journey into the AI ecosystem — starting with beads (bd) as the entry point. It reflects that exploration: experimenting with AI-assisted development, discovering what works, hitting walls, iterating. It was never meant to be a polished product — it was the lab notebook of that journey.

All the knowledge and lessons learned have now been carried forward into **PaiR** (https://pair.w3dev.fr), a complete rewrite that takes everything much further: multi-project dashboards, real-time AI activity tracking, epics & dependencies, GitHub/GitLab sync, attachments with preview, integrated terminals, and full cross-platform support (macOS, Linux, Windows).

If you'd like to follow along on that next chapter, you're very welcome to join as a PaiR user — feel free to reach out on the PaiR downloads page (https://pair.w3dev.fr).

And if you'd rather continue exploring on your own path with Beads Task Issue Tracker — I'd encourage you to fork the project! It's a great way to learn about Tauri, Nuxt, and the beads ecosystem, the same way I did. The codebase is well-documented and the architecture is straightforward.

Thanks again for taking the time to share this
